name: Perseid Meteor Shower
desc: The most popular meteor shower of the year. Up to 100 meteors per hour under dark skies. Best viewing: after midnight, northeast sky.

name: Total Solar Eclipse
desc: Totality crosses Greenland, Iceland, northern Spain (including Valencia and Palma), and northwestern Africa. Partial eclipse visible across much of Europe.

name: Orionid Meteor Shower
desc: Debris from Halley's Comet. Swift, bright meteors, some with persistent trains. Best viewing: after midnight.

name: Leonid Meteor Shower
desc: Known for occasional spectacular storms. Produces fast meteors (71 km/s) with bright fireballs. Every 33 years near the comet's perihelion it can reach thousands per hour.

name: Geminid Meteor Shower
desc: The richest and most reliable annual meteor shower. Unusually, debris comes from an asteroid rather than a comet. Multicolored meteors visible from early evening.

name: Quadrantid Meteor Shower
desc: One of the strongest showers but with a very sharp peak lasting only 6 hours. Best viewed from the northern hemisphere before dawn.

name: Lyrid Meteor Shower
desc: One of the oldest recorded meteor showers — Chinese astronomers noted it in 687 BCE. Occasionally produces brief outbursts up to 100/hr.

name: Eta Aquariid Meteor Shower
desc: Produced by Halley's Comet debris. Excellent from the Southern Hemisphere; northern observers see fewer but still fast meteors grazing the horizon at dawn.

name: Perseid Meteor Shower
desc: The most popular meteor shower of the year. Up to 100 meteors per hour. Best after midnight with Perseus rising in the northeast.

name: Annular Solar Eclipse
desc: The 'ring of fire' eclipse — the Moon is too far from Earth to cover the Sun completely, leaving a bright ring. Path crosses southern South America and the South Atlantic.

name: Total Solar Eclipse
desc: One of the longest total solar eclipses of the 21st century. Totality crosses Morocco, Algeria, Libya, Egypt, Saudi Arabia, Yemen, and Somalia. An exceptionally long event — nearly 6.5 minutes of darkness.

name: Mars at Opposition
desc: Mars is at its closest approach to Earth and fully illuminated by the Sun. Best time in years to observe Mars through a telescope — surface features and polar ice caps visible.

name: Jupiter at Opposition
desc: Jupiter rises at sunset and is visible all night. At closest approach, the four Galilean moons and cloud bands are easily seen in binoculars. Great Red Spot visible through a telescope.

name: Saturn at Opposition
desc: Saturn's rings are tilted toward Earth, making this an ideal time to observe. The ring system and at least four moons (including Titan) are visible through a small telescope.

name: Saturn Ring Plane Crossing
desc: Saturn's rings appear edge-on from Earth for the first time since 2009. The iconic rings become a thin line. A rare event occurring roughly every 15 years.

name: ISS Solar/Lunar Transits
desc: The International Space Station crosses the Sun or Moon in under 1 second — visible only from a narrow strip of ground. Use transit-finder.com to find exact times for your location.

name: Venus–Jupiter Conjunction
desc: Venus and Jupiter appear less than 0.5° apart in the evening sky — both visible simultaneously in a telescope's field of view. A spectacular naked-eye event.

name: Comet Watching Season
desc: Comets are unpredictable visitors. In recent years, C/2023 A3 (Tsuchinshan-ATLAS) put on a spectacular display. Check NASA's comet page for current brightest comets.
